content: The changes in seasons on Earth are caused primarily by the tilt of the planet’s axis, not by the distance from the sun. Earth’s axis is tilted about 23.5 degrees relative to its orbit around the sun, which means different parts of Earth get varying amounts of sunlight throughout the year. When the Northern Hemisphere tilts toward the sun, it receives more direct sunlight and experiences summer, while the Southern Hemisphere gets less direct sunlight and has winter. Sunlight hitting the Earth directly is more concentrated and delivers more energy, warming the surface more than when the sun is lower in the sky. Scientists have measured sunlight angles and temperatures worldwide, confirming that areas tilted toward the sun are warmer and have longer days during their summer months. In fact, Earth is closest to the sun during the Northern Hemisphere’s winter, which disproves the distance theory as the main cause of seasons. The consistent pattern of Earth’s tilt and orbit explains why seasons repeat every year naturally.
